I
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT I-{V
220 MONTGOMERY STREET, SUITE 8OO
SAN FRANCISCO, CAUFORNIA 94104
4r5/398.6230 FAX 415/398'7256
March 6, l-990
Kathryn Donovan, General counsel
Fair Po1itica1 Practices commission
P. O. Box 807
sacramento, California 9 5814 -08 07
Dear Ms- Donovan:
In the FPPCTS advice letter to Mr. Hiltachk, Decenber
1989, the Commission advised that contributions fron
candidate committees to candidate controlled baltot neasure
committees would vj-o1ate the transfer provision of
Proposition 73. As you know, several, of our clients
profoundly disagree with that interpretation and a challenge
to the entj-re transfer provision is currently pending in
United States District Court.
As a result of the prohibition legislative candidates
l.rho are very nuch interested in the reapportionment
initiatives 
have refrained fron rnaking transfers to candidate
controlled committees opposing Proposition 118 and 119. The
comrnittees are the Connittee Aqainst Reapportionrnent
Dishonesty, David Roberti, Chair and the Committee for Fai-r
Reapportionment, Willie L. Bro$rn, Jr., Chair. This has
created substantial problerns in the ability of the cornmittees
to raise seed funds and to oppose the initiatives.
We have just learned that the proponents of
Proposition 119 have been laboring under no such handicap.
The enclosed report by californians for Political Reform, Tom
Huening, Chairnan (I.D. No. 882493) shows that they have
received substantial funds fron, including, Becky Morgan
(i28,525.74), Bill Thomas ($5,000), Marion Bergeson ($zso1,
Pete Schabarun ($5,000), Ton Canpbell ($5,000), Marian
LaFollette ($rO,OOo) Ton Riley ($250), Deane Dana ($15,000),
BiII Duplissea ($7,000), Ron Packard ($2200), and Trice
Harvery ($2,000). The committee is controlled by Thomas
Huening, who is a nember of the San Mateo County Board of
Supervisors, a candidate for re-election and cl-early a
candidate within the meaning of the Political Reform Act.
Proposition 119 thus gualified with candidate
transferred funds, but other candidates on the other side of
the issue cannot transfer funds in order to lrork to defeat
it.
l{an 13 7 ls iii'30


Kathryn Donovan, General counsel
March 6, 1990
Page 2
Given the tenuous constitutionality of any
restriction in the first p1ace, we ask that the staff
reconsider its advice on this issue. Specifically, this is a
forrnal request for advi.ce on whether, in the following
circumstances, a candidate cornmittee may contribute funds to
the two bal1ot measure committees opposing Propositions 118
and 119 and controlled by Senator Roberti and speaker Brown,
respectively.
1. 
The candidate reasonably believes that such a
contribution will help his eLection prospects, in both the
coming year and future years and the expenditure is
reasonably related to a political, 
legislative or
governmental purpose. see Govrt code 585801.
2. 
The ballot committee uses no advertising in any
way to promote the candidacy of the controlling candidate and
makes no expenditures for the benefit of a candidate or
candidates.
3. 
The measure has qualified for the bal-Iot using
funds contributed by other candidates, notwithstanding the
f'PPc's position in Hiltachk.
ltoreover, to the extent that the staff advice was in
any way predicated on the trust doctrine of Proposition 73,
that provision has been amended by Senate Bill 1431 to
specifically permit any expenditure that is rrreasonabl-y
relatedt' to a political, 
legj-slative or governmental purpose
and would no longer act as a bar to such a transfer.
Thank you for your pronpt attention to this request.
Sincerely,
Jry1 &---Z
Joseph Remcho
JR: Inf

sections.question: Whether, in the following circumstances, a candidate committee may contribute funds to the two ballot measure committees opposing Propositions 118 and 119 and controlled by Senator Roberti and Speaker Brown, respectively: (1) The candidate reasonably believes that such a contribution will help his election prospects, in both the coming year and future years and the expenditure is reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose per Government Code §85801; (2) The ballot committee uses no advertising in any way to promote the candidacy of the controlling candidate and makes no expenditures for the benefit of a candidate or candidates; and (3) The measure has qualified for the ballot using funds contributed by other candidates, notwithstanding the FPPC's position in Hiltachk.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether candidate committees may contribute funds to ballot measure committees opposing Propositions 118 and 119 that are controlled by candidates (Senator Roberti and Speaker Brown), given that the ballot measures were qualified using funds from other candidates, the contributions would be reasonably related to political/legislative purposes, and the committees would not use advertising to promote the controlling candidates.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/formal letter asking FPPC to reconsider prior advice, not an FPPC response. No FPPC conclusion section present. Document discusses reapportionment initiatives (Propositions 118 and 119) and cites Government Code §85801 and Senate Bill 1431 amendments to Proposition 73's trust doctrine.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether, in the following circumstances, a candidate committee may contribute funds to the two ballot measure committees opposing Propositions 118 and 119 and controlled by Senator Roberti and Speaker Brown, respectively: (1) The candidate reasonably believes that such a contribution will help his election prospects, in both the coming year and future years and the expenditure is reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose per Government Code §85801; (2) The ballot committee uses no advertising in any way to promote the candidacy of the controlling candidate and makes no expenditures for the benefit of a candidate or candidates; and (3) The measure has qualified for the ballot using funds contributed by other candidates, notwithstanding the FPPC's position in Hiltachk.

embedding.summary: An incoming request letter from Remcho, Johansen & Purcell challenging a prior FPPC advice letter (Hiltachk, December 1989) that prohibited candidate committee contributions to candidate-controlled ballot measure committees, and formally requesting reconsideration of that advice based on constitutional concerns and the application of Government Code §85801.
